exercises.title: Evaluation as the outer loop
exercises.prompt: Anthropic's guidance: 'start with simple prompts, optimize them with comprehensive evaluation, and add multi-step agentic systems only when needed.' Explain why evaluation is the outer loop (not the last step), and name the three evaluation layers (static benchmarks, custom offline, online production) and what each is for.
exercises.hint: Eval is the outer loop because it DRIVES every choice — you measure, then decide (better prompt? add an agent? change model?). Three layers: static benchmarks (public, rough prior), custom offline (your tasks, before deploy), online production (real traffic, after deploy). Each catches different things.
exercises.solution:
**Why evaluation is the outer loop (not the last step):** the common (wrong) view treats evaluation as **the final QA step** — build the agent, then test it at the end. Anthropic's guidance inverts this: **evaluation is the *outer loop* that drives every other choice.** You **start with a clear eval, and it gates every decision:**
- 'Start with simple prompts' → measure with eval → is it good enough? 
- If not, 'optimize them with comprehensive evaluation' → the eval **tells you what to improve** and **whether your change helped.**
- 'Add multi-step agentic systems only when needed' → the eval **shows you when a simpler approach is insufficient** (justifying complexity, the simple-first doctrine, Lessons 12, 28).
So eval isn't a step *in* the process — it's the **loop that *drives* the process.** Every choice (prompt, model, framework, topology, whether to add an agent) is **made based on what the eval shows** — you change something, measure, and the measurement decides the next move. Evaluation is the **steering wheel**, not the final inspection. This is why it's 'the outer loop that drives every choice in Phase 14' — without it, you're guessing (and 'reading 10 outputs and shipping' is hope, not engineering, Phase 11.10).

**The three evaluation layers:**
1. **Static benchmarks (public):** SWE-bench, GAIA, AgentBench (Lessons 19-20) — **public, fixed benchmarks**. *For:* a **rough capability prior** — comparing models/approaches at a high level, knowing roughly where the field is. *Limits:* they measure narrow slices, are contaminated/saturated, and **don't measure your task** (Lessons 19-20). Use as a starting prior, not the answer.
2. **Custom offline (your tasks, before deploy):** **your own eval set** — representative + edge + adversarial cases from *your* domain, with *your* success criteria, run **offline before deploying** (Phase 10.10, 11.10). *For:* measuring whether the agent works **on your actual task** — the trustworthy signal for development and ship/no-ship decisions. **The most important layer** ('the only eval that matters is YOUR eval', Phase 10.10). Decontaminated, private.
3. **Online production (real traffic, after deploy):** **evals on live production traffic** — sample real requests, run evals on them, monitor quality/cost/latency over time (Phase 11.10, Lesson 24). *For:* catching **production drift and real-world failures** that offline eval misses (the input distribution shifts, the model updates, real users do unexpected things). Catches what offline can't (reality).
**Why three layers:** they catch **different things** — static benchmarks give a **prior** (rough capability), custom offline gives **pre-deploy confidence on your task** (the development signal), and online production gives **post-deploy reality** (drift/real-world). Together they cover the agent's lifecycle: **prior → pre-deploy → in-production.** You need all three, with **custom offline as the development driver** and **online as the production safety net** (Phase 11.10's layered eval, now for agents).

exercises.title: The evaluator-optimizer loop and evals in CI
exercises.prompt: Explain the evaluator-optimizer tight loop and describe the 2026 best practice: evals live next to code, run in CI, gate PRs. Implement a simple CI eval gate that blocks a change if it regresses the eval. Why does gating PRs on evals matter for agents?
exercises.hint: Evaluator-optimizer: generate → evaluate → optimize → re-evaluate (tight loop, Lesson 5). Best practice: eval files in the repo, run automatically in CI on every PR, block merge if scores regress. CI gate: run eval, compare to baseline, fail if below threshold. Matters because agent changes silently regress (Phase 11.10) — CI catches it before merge.
exercises.solution:
**The evaluator-optimizer tight loop:** the inner loop of eval-driven development (the Self-Refine / evaluator-optimizer pattern, Lesson 5, Phase 11.5/12): **generate → evaluate → optimize → re-evaluate**, iterating:
1. **Generate** a version (a prompt, an agent config).
2. **Evaluate** it against the eval set (custom offline, Exercise 1) — measure quality.
3. **Optimize** based on the eval results — change what the eval revealed as weak.
4. **Re-evaluate** — did the change improve the metric? 
This **tight loop** lets you **iterate rapidly with a trustworthy signal** — each change is **measured**, so you keep improvements and discard regressions. The eval is the **fitness function** driving the optimization (like AlphaEvolve's loop, Lesson 11, but for agent quality). Without the eval in the loop, you're optimizing blind.

**The 2026 best practice (evals next to code, run in CI, gate PRs):** treat evals like **tests** (the prompts-are-code principle, Phase 11.1):
- **Evals live next to code** — eval datasets and eval scripts are **in the repo** (versioned with the code), not in a separate notebook or someone's head. They're a first-class part of the codebase.
- **Run in CI** — evals run **automatically in CI on every change** (every PR), like unit tests — no manual 'remember to eval.'
- **Gate PRs** — a PR is **blocked from merging if it regresses the eval** (drops below a threshold or below the baseline). Quality regressions are caught **before they merge**, automatically.

**A simple CI eval gate:**
```python
def ci_eval_gate(agent, eval_set, baseline_score, threshold=0.02):
    score = run_eval(agent, eval_set)              # run the eval suite
    print(f"eval score: {score:.3f} (baseline: {baseline_score:.3f})")
    if score < baseline_score - threshold:         # regression beyond tolerance
        print("BLOCKED: this change regresses the eval")
        exit(1)                                     # fail CI -> block the PR merge
    print("PASSED: no regression")
    exit(0)
# Runs in CI on every PR; fails (blocks merge) if the change drops the eval score.
```
The gate **runs the eval, compares to the baseline, and fails CI (blocking the merge) if the score regresses** beyond a tolerance — exactly like a failing test blocks a merge (the eval-gate pattern, Phase 10.13, 11.10). Add **statistical significance** (Phase 11.10 — require the regression to exceed noise) and **cost-gating** (Phase 13.20 — run the full eval on PRs to main, cheap checks on every commit).

**Why gating PRs on evals matters for agents:** because **agent changes silently regress** (Phase 11.10's core insight, amplified for agents):
- **Any change — a prompt tweak, a model swap, a new tool, a topology change — shifts the agent's behavior unpredictably** (the prompts-are-code fragility, Phase 11.1), and **agents fail silently** (fluent wrong outputs, no crash, Phase 11.10). So a regression is **invisible without measurement** — you'd ship it and not know.
- **Gating PRs on evals catches regressions *before they merge*** — automatically, on every change, before the regression reaches production. It turns 'I think this change is better' into 'CI confirms it doesn't regress (or blocks it if it does).' 
- **It makes agent development safe to iterate** — you can change prompts/models/tools confidently because **CI will catch any regression**, just like tests make code safe to refactor. Without it, every change is a gamble and quality erodes silently (the team that 'reads 10 outputs and ships' ships regressions they never see, Phase 11.10).
- **Agents have huge surface area** (prompts, tools, memory, topology) and **non-deterministic behavior** — making regressions especially easy to introduce and hard to spot manually. **Automated eval gating is the only scalable way** to maintain quality across an agent's many components.
Gating PRs on evals is the **production-engineering discipline** that makes eval-driven development *real* — evals as CI tests, blocking regressions automatically — turning evaluation from an aspiration into **enforced infrastructure** (the same discipline as Phase 11.10's CI/CD eval integration, here as the core of eval-driven agent development). It's what lets a team iterate on agents fast *and* safely.

exercises.title: Eval-driven development as the unifying discipline of Phase 14
exercises.prompt: Explain how eval-driven development unifies the whole phase (it gates every choice: prompts, patterns, frameworks, topology, the workbench), why 'start simple, add complexity only when the eval shows you need it' is the through-line, the challenge of evaluating agents (vs single outputs — trajectory, multi-step, non-determinism), and how this connects to observability (Lesson 24) and the workbench (Lessons 31-42).
exercises.hint: Eval-driven gates EVERY choice in the phase — it's the meta-principle behind 'simple over complex' (you add complexity only when the eval proves it's needed). Evaluating agents is hard: judge the whole trajectory not one output, multi-step, non-deterministic, expensive. Connects to observability (eval ON traces) and the workbench (verification gates ARE eval-driven).
exercises.solution:
**How eval-driven development unifies the whole phase:** eval-driven development is the **meta-discipline that gates every choice in Phase 14:**
- **Reasoning patterns (Lessons 2-5):** use eval to decide when CoT/ReWOO/reflection/search is worth it (do they improve the metric? — Lessons 2, 4's 'when is it worth the cost').
- **Memory/skills (Lessons 7-10):** eval whether richer memory/skills improve outcomes (do they help on your task?).
- **Frameworks (Lessons 13-18):** eval which framework/approach performs best for your task (Phase 11.17's match-the-abstraction, verified by measurement).
- **Orchestration/topology (Lessons 25, 28):** eval whether a multi-agent topology *earns its complexity* (does it solve something a simpler shape can't, by enough to justify the cost? — Lesson 28's core test).
- **The workbench (Lessons 31-42):** eval whether the workbench surfaces improve reliability (Lesson 41's prompt-only-vs-workbench comparison — 'the numbers do the arguing').
**Every decision in the phase is made by measurement** — eval-driven development is the **unifying loop** that all the other lessons feed into. It's not one topic among many; it's the **outer loop that drives the use of every other topic** (Exercise 1). The phase teaches many techniques (patterns, memory, frameworks, topology, the workbench), and **eval is how you decide which to use, when, and whether they help.**

**Why 'start simple, add complexity only when the eval shows you need it' is the through-line:** this is the **phase's most repeated principle** (Lessons 2, 12, 15, 28, 30) — and **eval-driven development is what makes it *operational*:**
- The principle says: **default to simple; add complexity (agents, topology, memory) only when needed.** 
- But **'when needed' is defined by the eval** — you **add complexity only when the eval shows the simpler approach is insufficient** (it fails on your task), and you **verify the complexity actually helps** (the eval improves). 
So eval-driven development is the **mechanism behind 'simple over complex'** — it's the **objective test that gates complexity.** Without eval, 'add complexity only when needed' is subjective (you'd over-engineer based on vibes); **with eval, 'needed' is *measured*** (the simple approach demonstrably fails, and the complex one demonstrably helps). The through-line — **start simple, escalate only by measured need** — *requires* eval-driven development to enforce it. Eval is what keeps you honest about complexity.

**The challenge of evaluating agents (vs single outputs):** evaluating agents is **harder than evaluating single LLM outputs** (Phase 11.10):
- **Trajectory, not just output:** an agent produces a **multi-step trajectory** (think → act → observe → ... → result), not one output. You must evaluate **the whole trajectory** — did it take the right steps, use tools correctly, stay in scope, recover from errors — not just the final answer (which could be right by luck or wrong despite a good process).
- **Multi-step success criteria:** success depends on a **sequence of correct actions** (did it complete the *task*, verified by execution? — like SWE-bench's test-gating, Lesson 19). Binary task-completion over a trajectory, not a single-output score.
- **Non-determinism:** agents are **highly non-deterministic** (sampling, tool variability) — the same task can produce different trajectories — so evals are **noisy** and need **multiple runs + statistical significance** (Phase 11.10).
- **Expensive:** running an agent (many LLM calls, tool executions) per eval case is **costly** — so agent evals are expensive to run (cost-gating, Phase 13.20).
- **Hard to grade:** evaluating a trajectory (was the *process* good?) often needs **execution-based checks** (did the task actually get done? — WebArena/SWE-bench style, Lessons 19-20) or careful LLM-judge rubrics (Phase 11.10), not simple matching.
So agent eval needs **trajectory-level, execution-based, multi-run, significance-tested** evaluation — much more involved than scoring a single text output. (This is why execution-based benchmarks like SWE-bench/WebArena are valued, Lessons 19-20.)

**Connection to observability (Lesson 24) and the workbench (Lessons 31-42):**
- **Observability (Lessons 23-24):** eval-driven development **runs on observability** — you **evaluate the *traced* agent runs** (the trajectory captured by tracing). Observability provides the **data** (what the agent did — the trajectory, Lesson 23); eval provides the **judgment** (was it good?). **Online evals** (Exercise 1's third layer) run **on production traces** via observability platforms (Lesson 24). Eval and observability are **two halves of one system** (Lesson 24): observability captures the trajectory, eval scores it — together driving the development loop.
- **The workbench (Lessons 31-42):** the workbench's **verification gates (Lesson 38) and reviewer agent (Lesson 39) ARE eval-driven development operationalized at the task level** — they're **automated evals on the agent's work** that **gate whether the task is done** (the agent doesn't mark its own work done; a verification gate / reviewer evaluates it). The workbench is, in effect, **eval-driven development built into the agent's runtime** — every task is evaluated (verification gate) before being accepted, and the reviewer is a second-loop evaluator. **Eval-driven development at the development level (CI eval gates, Exercise 2) and the workbench's verification at the task level (Lesson 38) are the same principle** — *the work is not done until an independent evaluation says so* — applied at different granularities (per-change in CI, per-task in the workbench). 
The synthesis: **eval-driven development is the unifying discipline of Phase 14** — it's the **outer loop that gates every choice** (patterns, memory, frameworks, topology, workbench), the **mechanism that operationalizes 'simple over complex'** (add complexity only when the eval proves it's needed), and it **runs on observability** (eval the traced trajectories) and **manifests in the workbench** (verification gates as task-level evals). Evaluating agents is **harder than single outputs** (trajectory, multi-step, non-deterministic, expensive) — requiring execution-based, multi-run, significance-tested evaluation — but it's **non-negotiable**: without it, agent development is guessing, complexity is unjustified, and regressions ship silently. **Eval is the steering wheel of agent engineering** — every other technique in the phase is *used* under the direction of evaluation, and the discipline of measuring-everything-and-adding-complexity-only-by-proven-need is what separates reliable agent engineering from hopeful prompt-tweaking. The whole phase is, in the end, **eval-driven**.
